nativeRange.habitat: The Golden Polyp Gorgonian is found on tropical Indo-Pacific reef slopes, typically at depths of 5–100 m, anchored to hard substrate in current-swept areas. Aquarium specimens are most commonly wild-collected from shallower reefs off Indonesia and Vietnam.

decorPreferences: live rock base for anchoring with underwater epoxy or reef glue
positioned upright in a high-flow zone
open water-column placement with several centimeters of spacing from neighboring corals
lower to middle rockwork zone preferred (NPS interpretation) or middle to upper for keepers treating it as photosynthetic

diet.notes: The Golden Polyp Gorgonian is best kept as a non-photosynthetic (NPS) filter feeder, requiring frequent offerings of small-particle foods — rotifers, cyclops, baby brine shrimp, lobster eggs, Marine Snow, powdered coral foods, and phytoplankton blends. Three to five feedings per week is the generally recommended minimum, with flow reduced briefly during feeding so the extended polyps can capture particles. Some trade retailers describe the species as photosynthetic and suggest only occasional supplemental feeding, but specialist and scientific sources place Menella firmly in the NPS category; the frequent-feeding approach is considered the safer husbandry choice (see careNotes for a fuller discussion of this discrepancy).

goodWith: reef fish that do not nip at polyps
seahorses (this species is commonly recommended for seahorse displays)
non-stinging soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive stinging corals placed in direct contact
LPS and SPS corals without adequate spacing
large hermit crabs or crabs that may climb and damage branches
angelfishes, butterflyfishes, or other fish known to nip at gorgonian polyps

summary: The Golden Polyp Gorgonian (Menella sp.) is an Indo-Pacific sea fan prized for its bright yellow polyps on upright branching colonies. It is generally considered an advanced-care species, as it is non-photosynthetic and depends on frequent small-particle feedings to survive in captivity.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT — photosynthetic status is disputed. Some trade retailers describe the Golden Polyp Gorgonian as photosynthetic and rate it as intermediate-care. Specialist hobby sources and scientific classification describe Menella as azooxanthellate (lacking the symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae, that photosynthetic corals rely on), placing it firmly in the NPS (non-photosynthetic) category and rating it as an advanced coral. The genus Menella belongs to family Acanthogorgiidae, a family dominated by azooxanthellate Indo-Pacific gorgonians, which lends taxonomic weight to the NPS reading. The husbandry guidance below follows the NPS approach, which specialist sources consider the safer choice for long-term colony health.

Key husbandry guidance:

• Flow: Strong, non-laminar (chaotic or surge-type) flow is essential. Branches should sway visibly. Insufficient flow tends to promote cyanobacteria and algae overgrowth on the colony base. Target high turnover (~20–40× tank volume per hour) with alternating or gyre-type circulation.
• Lighting: Light is not required for nutrition, but the colony tolerates moderate reef lighting. Low-to-moderate light is sufficient and may reduce nuisance algae fouling.
• Feeding: Critical. Offer small-particle plankton foods (rotifers, cyclops, lobster eggs, Marine Snow, baby brine shrimp, phytoplankton, powdered coral foods) 3–5+ times per week. Reduce flow briefly during feeding so polyps can capture particles. Insufficient feeding is widely reported as the most common cause of decline for this species in captivity.
• Mounting: Anchor the base with aquarium-safe epoxy or reef glue to live rock. Position upright in a high-flow zone. Branches are brittle — handle with care during placement. A healthy colony will encrust its mounting rock over time.
• Cyanobacteria / algae risk: Like other gorgonians, Menella is reported to be susceptible to cyanobacteria fouling on the base and lower branches when flow is inadequate. Gently brush with a soft brush if observed. Coral dips and freshwater dips are generally not recommended for gorgonians, as keepers have reported sensitivity to dipping.
• Water chemistry: Maintain stable reef parameters — calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, alkalinity 8–11 dKH. Low-to-moderate nutrient levels appear well tolerated; a small amount of phosphate and nitrate may actually benefit growth, which is consistent with NPS feeding habits.
• Aggression: The Golden Polyp Gorgonian is non-aggressive and does not produce sweeper tentacles. It is, however, vulnerable to stings from neighboring LPS (large-polyp stony) and SPS (small-polyp stony) corals, so maintain several centimeters of spacing around the colony.
• Trade variants: The trade label 'Menella' is sometimes applied to white, yellow, gold, red, and purple color variants. Color appears to reflect intraspecific or cross-species variation within the genus; husbandry is the same across colors. Wild colonies have been reported to reach approximately 50 cm in height.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation (fragging) is the primary captive propagation method. Branches can be cut and attached to frag plugs or rubble with underwater epoxy or cyanoacrylate gel; the colony encrusts its mount over time. Sexual reproduction in this genus is not commonly documented in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Marine World Aquatics – Golden Polyp Gorgonia (Menella spp)
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Describes Menella spp as photosynthetic and intermediate-to-challenging care; recommends supplemental feeding several times per week with small-particle foods. Notes susceptibility to cyanobacteria and algae overgrowth. No native-range, depth, or numeric water-parameter data given on the product page itself. Substituted for LiveAquaria as the manifest's primary because LiveAquaria has no current Menella product page (their catalogue carries Muricea elongata / Rusty Gorgonian and Yellow Finger Gorgonian instead).

additional.name: peerintoyourworld – Menella sp. (Gorgonian) species page
additional.notes: Authoritative species reference. States Menella sp. is found in the tropical Indo-Pacific at 5–100 m depth on reef slopes, reaches up to 50 cm in height, has sparsely branched fan/bush morphology, and crucially LACKS zooxanthellae — feeding entirely on planktonic food filtered from the water column. This directly contradicts the trade-source photosynthetic claim. Used as the authoritative source for nativeRange, depthRangeM, adultSizeCm, and the NPS husbandry stance.

additional.name: WoRMS – Menella Gray, 1870 (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomic placement only. Confirms genus Menella in family Acanthogorgiidae, order Malacalcyonacea, class Octocorallia. Type species is M. indica Gray, 1870; genus contains 32 accepted species including M. praelonga, M. indica, M. filiformis, M. simplex. Acanthogorgiidae is the family of predominantly azooxanthellate Indo-Pacific gorgonians, lending taxonomic weight to the NPS reading.

additional.name: Lifestock – Menella Yellow / Gold Gorgonian
additional.notes: Cross-checked trade size and feeding guidance. Frag sizes S (3–4 cm), M (5–7 cm), L (8–10 cm). Recommends T5 or high-end LED lighting and dust food / plankton supplemental feeding. Describes as 'easy to keep, even for beginners' — disagrees with peerintoyourworld and Reef2Reef forum consensus. Notes the species tolerates and may benefit from small amounts of phosphate and nitrate (consistent with NPS feeding). Also suitable for seahorse aquariums.

additional.name: Tropical Marine Centre (TMC) – Golden Polyp Gorgonia (Menelia spp.)
additional.notes: Wholesale distributor reference. States the species is planktivorous, intermediate-care, with medium light requirements and reef-safe; recommends a plankton-rich diet (Gamma Foods range). TMC spells the genus 'Menelia' (apparent typo for Menella). No numeric water-parameter data on the page.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog – Gorgonian Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Used to cross-verify general gorgonian water parameters: temperature 73–84°F (23–29°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, high flow recommended for all sea fans, feeding ≥3× weekly with zooplankton foods. Explicitly classifies NPS gorgonians as 'expert-only; not recommended for most tanks' — supporting the advanced careLevel set in this entry.
